persons.arguments_against: The strongest case against: the corpus holds 4 Skeen officials and 2 Skeen biographies, so the stint could belong to a relative (e.g. a son) working in the same Ceylon printing establishment, and all five bio events inherit their place from a single 'Ceylon' clause, which the deterministic pass flags as weak. This fails because the staff list prints the full three-initial form 'G. J. A. Skeen' in every one of 15 edition-years, matching the biography's initials exactly; the office of Government Printer of Ceylon is a unique, non-generic post held by one person at a time; and the career shape matches in detail — the bio has him assistant govt. printer from 1872 and government printer from 1880/1881, while the staff list shows 'Assistant Government Printer' 1877-1879 and 'Government Printer' from 1883 onward (the 1880 'Government Secretary' row is a transcription slip in an otherwise unbroken printer sequence). The bio is reprinted in editions 1886, 1889, 1898-1906, the same editions in which the stint lists him as Government Printer. A relative would need the same three initials and the same unique office simultaneously, which is not realistic.
persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second 'G. J. A. Skeen' in Ceylon (e.g. a son with identical initials in the printing department), or a printed record showing this biography's subject left Ceylon or died before 1906 while the staff listing continued.

notes: Single-bio, single-stint case. Three-initial match plus the unique office of Government Printer, Ceylon, with assistant-to-principal career progression mirrored in both sources and 2 agreeing edition co-occurrences. The 1880 staff-list row reading 'Government Secretary' is almost certainly an OCR or compositor error for 'Government Printer' (the bio says acting government printer 1875, government printer 1880).
